name: Herbal Archetypes: Universal Plant Medicine Roles

related_content.title: Related Content
points: Earth Mother - Source of plant life
Death God - Poisonous and psychoactive plants
Spiritual Items - Sacred objects and tools

keyMyths.title: The Creation of Plants (Various Cultures)
keyMyths.description: Across many cultures, plants are believed to have sprung from the earth, often imbued with the life force of deities or primordial beings. These origin stories connect plants directly to the divine and establish their inherent power and healing properties.
keyMyths.source: Frazer, James George. *The Golden Bough: A Study in Magic and Religion*.

keyMyths.title: The Healing Herbs of Asclepius (Greek)
keyMyths.description: Asclepius, the Greek god of medicine, learned the secrets of healing from various plants, often guided by animals or divine intervention. His knowledge of herbal remedies allowed him to cure the sick and even raise the dead, highlighting the transformative potential of plant medicine.

keyMyths.title: The Legend of Shennong (Chinese)
keyMyths.description: Shennong, the Yan Emperor, is a legendary figure in Chinese mythology credited with discovering agriculture and herbal medicine. He tasted hundreds of plants to determine their medicinal properties, often suffering poisoning in the process, demonstrating the dedication and sacrifice required to understand plant medicine.
keyMyths.source: *Shennong Ben Cao Jing (The Divine Farmer's Herb-Root Classic)*

keyMyths.title: The Mistletoe and Balder (Norse)
keyMyths.description: In Norse mythology, Balder, the beloved god, could only be harmed by mistletoe. This myth showcases the dual nature of plants: capable of both healing and causing harm, depending on their application and knowledge of their properties.

keyMyths.title: The Tree of Life (Various Cultures)
keyMyths.description: The Tree of Life, a recurring motif in many mythologies, symbolizes the interconnectedness of all living things and the life-sustaining power of nature, including plants. It often represents access to wisdom, healing, and immortality through the consumption or understanding of its fruits or leaves.
keyMyths.source: Mircea Eliade. *The Sacred and the Profane*.

sources.name: Frazer, James George. *The Golden Bough: A Study in Magic and Religion*
sources.description: A comprehensive study of comparative mythology and religion, exploring the role of plants in rituals and beliefs across cultures.
sources.title: Frazer, James George. *The Golden Bough: A Study in Magic and Religion*

sources.name: Homer. *The Iliad*
sources.description: An ancient Greek epic poem that references the healing properties of plants and the skills of healers.
sources.title: Homer. *The Iliad*

sources.name: *Shennong Ben Cao Jing (The Divine Farmer's Herb-Root Classic)*
sources.description: Considered the oldest Chinese pharmacopoeia, documenting the medicinal properties of hundreds of herbs.
sources.title: *Shennong Ben Cao Jing (The Divine Farmer's Herb-Root Classic)*

sources.name: Snorri Sturluson. *The Prose Edda*
sources.description: A key source of Norse mythology, providing accounts of gods, heroes, and the creation of the world, including the story of Balder and the mistletoe.
sources.title: Snorri Sturluson. *The Prose Edda*

sources.name: Mircea Eliade. *The Sacred and the Profane*
sources.description: A seminal work in the study of religion, exploring the concept of the sacred and its manifestation in various cultures, including the symbolism of plants and trees.
sources.title: Mircea Eliade. *The Sacred and the Profane*

canonical: herbal archetypes: universal plant medicine roles
